summary:
The Khazar Khaganate (c. 650–969 AD) was a major Turkic state that dominated the Pontic-Caspian steppe between the Caucasus Mountains and the Volga River, controlling the critical land routes connecting Eastern Europe with Central Asia and the Byzantine and Islamic worlds. At its height, Khazaria controlled territory stretching from the Black Sea to the Aral Sea, extracting tribute from surrounding peoples and taxing the enormous trade flows passing through its territory — silk, furs, slaves, amber, and spices moving between Scandinavia, Byzantium, Persia, and China.

The Khazars are most remarkable in world history for the conversion of their ruling elite to Judaism — possibly in the 8th or 9th century — making them one of the very few polities in history to adopt Judaism as a state religion outside a Jewish ethnic context. The reasons for this conversion remain debated: some sources suggest it was a deliberate political choice to remain neutral between the competing Christian Byzantine Empire and Islamic Abbasid Caliphate, both of whom pressured the Khazars to convert. The Khaganate played a decisive strategic role in halting the northward expansion of Islam, defeating Arab forces twice (in 642 and 737 AD) in the battles for the Caucasus passes, effectively protecting the Slavic and Eastern European peoples from Islamization. Khazaria collapsed under sustained pressure from the Rus' under Sviatoslav I, who sacked its capital Atil on the Volga around 969 AD.
